w:r.w:t: UAT checks the functionality, usability, accuracy, efficiency, and acceptability of the POS and Inventory Management System developed for Maramag Fish Landing. This test involved eight respondents, namely the different fish brokers, staff, and personnel in the LEEO, representatives of the actual users. The main objective of UAT is to appraise whether the system meets user requirements and is fit for deployment in the real world.

The evaluation was carried out using a 5-point Likert scale figure 32, where 5 indicates Strongly Agree and 1 indicates Strongly Disagree. The respondents assessed the system based on five key criteria: Functionality, Usability, Accuracy, Efficiency, and Overall Satisfaction.

In table 13, Usability criterion had a mean score of 4.63, meaning that users felt the system was easy to understand and navigate. The outcome reveals that interface design, menu structure, and workflow of the system are appropriate for brokers and staff operating in a fast-moving environment.

Among the criteria evaluated, Accuracy got the highest mean score of 4.88 in table 14, which supports that the system records sales transactions accurately, updates inventory levels, and tracks fish box movements through QR codes. It further confers that the system generates reliable and consistent data during daily activities.

In table 15, Efficiency recorded an average score of 4.50, which indicates that the system reduced manual work and increased efficiency in sales recording and inventory tracking as compared to the old manual procedure. The respondents still rated it as Strongly Acceptable, but they suggested some minor enhancements to further improve the flow and ease of use of the system.

In table 16 Functionality and table 17 Overall Satisfaction both provided a mean score of 4.75 to show that the system core activities, which include user login, sales creation, QR code scanning, payment management, receipt printing, and report generation functioned well. The users are satisfied with the performance of the overall system and ascertain that it enhances their operation at the fish landing.

The computed result shows that the overall mean score for the system is 4.70, which verbally interprets to be strongly acceptable. This means that the system was able to meet user requirements and showed good performance during actual use.

The results of User Acceptance Testing confirm that the Point-of-Sale and Inventory Management System for Maramag Fish Landing is highly acceptable to its prospective users. The system satisfactorily supports daily sales transactions, monitoring of inventory, fish boxes, and administrative oversight. While there were some minor suggestions for enhancement, general feedback revealed that the system is reliable, user-friendly, and ready to be deployed at Maramag Fish Landing.

The implemented results show that the developed system was able to address the problems identified in the earlier chapters of the study. The manual process previously used in Maramag Fish Landing was characterized by slow recording, weak fish box accountability, and difficulty in monitoring balances and reports. Through system development, these concerns were addressed by providing structured digital modules for sales processing, payment monitoring, fish box tracking, and administrative reporting.

The sales module addressed the problem of incomplete and inconsistent manual sales recording by organizing sales information into structured transaction records. The payment module improved accountability in buyer balances by systematically storing payment records and computing remaining amounts. The fish box module and QR code functionality reduced errors in fish box identification and strengthened monitoring of box status. The dashboards and reports improved operational transparency by providing timely access to summarized information for both brokers and administrative users.

The results therefore indicate that the developed system was not only technically functional but also operationally relevant to the workflow of Maramag Fish Landing. The system features were aligned with the actual needs identified during interviews and observation, which supports the appropriateness of the developed solution.
